identity.title: Administration of Human Resource Programs (except Education, Public Health, and Veterans' Affairs Programs)

lens.included_activities: U.S. federal, state, and local government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating public-assistance, social-work, welfare, Social Security, disability-insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, unemployment-insurance, workers'-compensation, food-assistance, and related human-resource programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Education, public-health, and veterans'-affairs program administration; direct operation of social-assistance services; private benefit administrators and software vendors; employment placement offices; schools, hospitals, clinics, charities, insurers, and captive units classified elsewhere; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Government agencies determine eligibility, enroll beneficiaries, calculate and issue or oversee benefits, manage appeals, verify data, prevent improper payments, coordinate programs, and monitor providers or state partners. They are funded through appropriations and taxes rather than recurring commercial fees from external customers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default LMM outsourced-service firm lens is structurally inapplicable because Census defines this code as government establishments, the frozen dataset has no receipts concept, and no defensible firm count exists. The packet preserves the full public-program administration population to assess workflow exposure and durable need, while marking firm-transfer and commercial-retention constructs missing rather than importing private vendors from other NAICS codes.

a.bridge: BLS describes eligibility interviewers as determining whether people qualify for welfare, unemployment, Social Security, housing, and other government assistance and reports 150,190 such workers across all industries in May 2023. GAO documents electronic verification using more than 30 data sources across six low-income programs and opportunities for data matching and AI in fraud and improper-payment work. I translate these cross-program occupations and digital control activities into wage-weighted exposure for 923130, emphasizing document intake, identity and income verification, case routing, calculation, correspondence, call summarization, data matching, anomaly detection, and reporting. The proxy may overstate substitution where interviews, disability evidence, appeals, or vulnerable applicants require judgment and understate it in high-volume rules-based programs.
a.rationale: AI can classify applications and evidence, extract documents, check completeness, match records, explain requirements, draft notices, summarize contacts, prioritize cases, detect duplicate or anomalous claims, translate communications, and assist program analysis. Statutory interpretation, adverse-action review, disability and hardship judgment, appeals, safeguarding due process, fraud investigation, and accountable payment authorization remain human-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted task inventory by program and government level, with time studies for intake, verification, adjudication, appeals, contact centers, fraud controls, policy, and oversight plus current automation penetration, would materially change the range.
caveats: Eligibility interviewers are only one occupation and the BLS estimate spans government and private employers rather than NAICS 923130 alone.
The frozen dataset provides no receipts denominator, so exposed work cannot be converted into a conventional private-firm labor-share opportunity.

rho.rationale: Rules-based intake, verification, correspondence, translation, and case prioritization can be implemented within five years, and agencies already use electronic data matching. Realization is constrained by federal-state-local fragmentation, old benefits systems, procurement, data quality, identity resolution, privacy, cybersecurity, accessibility, explainability, discrimination risk, statutory notice and appeal requirements, false positives, workforce training, vendor dependence, and the need for a human in the loop. Backlogs may cause capacity gains to improve service rather than reduce employment.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments across Social Security, SNAP, Medicaid, unemployment insurance, and workers' compensation reporting coverage, accuracy by demographic group, false-positive and appeal rates, caseworker time, processing speed, system cost, human overrides, and sustained adoption would narrow the estimate.
caveats: Implementation economics differ sharply among federal entitlement systems, state-administered programs, and small local welfare offices.
Error reduction and backlog clearance are valuable but are not necessarily labor substitution or avoided hiring.

e.rationale: There is no defensible share of LMM firms eligible under the default lens because the industry contains government establishments, the frozen dataset supplies no firm count, and public-benefit agencies do not have normalized EBITDA or transferable private control.
e.change_evidence: A formally adopted public-sector screening methodology with an explicit unit of analysis, eligibility denominator, and replacement for EBITDA and private control would be required before this primitive could be estimated.
caveats: Private eligibility, call-center, systems-integration, and claims-administration contractors belong to other NAICS codes.
Counting agencies or local offices as firms would be a category error rather than a conservative approximation.

s5.rationale: Agency reorganizations, elections, appointments, program transfers, consolidations, and vendor recompetes are not qualifying sales of control in eligible private operating companies. With no eligible-firm denominator, a five-year transfer probability is undefined.
s5.change_evidence: A replacement public-sector construct specifying which reorganizations, service concessions, or privatizations qualify and providing a measurable denominator would be necessary; ordinary M&A and owner-succession data cannot answer this primitive.
caveats: A contract award can transfer service delivery without transferring ownership of the government agency.
Program administration may move among agencies or government levels while public accountability remains unchanged.

q.rationale: These agencies are funded by appropriations and taxes, not customer fees, commercial renewals, or market pricing. There is therefore no defensible share of gross benefit retained after customer pass-through and price competition under the primitive's commercial definition.
q.change_evidence: A methodology replacing commercial retention with a defined public-value or budget-retention measure, including treatment of reduced improper payments, faster service, reappropriation, and quality gains, would be required.
caveats: Avoided improper payments accrue to public budgets and program integrity, not operating profit.
Administrative savings may be absorbed by backlogs, new mandates, or benefit-service improvements rather than retained by the agency.

d5.bridge: Census reports that the population age 65 and older grew 3.1% to 61.2 million from 2023 to 2024, supporting rising Social Security and Medicare administration, while BLS reported a 4.2% unemployment rate and 7.1 million unemployed people in June 2026, showing a large but cyclical unemployment-program workload. USDA continues to publish national and state SNAP participation and benefit data through fiscal 2025. I translate these demographic and program-demand indicators into five-year real demand for the broad current administration basket. Aging likely pushes the proxy upward, but economic conditions, eligibility rules, benefit redesign, agency budgets, and digitization can move workloads independently of population, so the range includes near-flat demand.
d5.rationale: Population aging, disability and retirement claims, income volatility, complex eligibility rules, fraud controls, appeals, and intergovernmental oversight sustain demand. Low unemployment, policy retrenchment, simplified eligibility, cross-program integration, better data sharing, and self-service can reduce transactions or administrative effort per beneficiary.
d5.change_evidence: Program-by-program five-year forecasts of applicants, beneficiaries, redeterminations, appeals, contact volume, payment transactions, fraud reviews, and real administrative spending for Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, SNAP, unemployment insurance, and workers' compensation would materially improve the estimate.
caveats: Older-population growth does not map one-for-one to administrative workload, and SNAP and unemployment caseloads are policy- and cycle-sensitive.
The code combines programs with opposing demand trends, so one national ratio obscures substantial heterogeneity.

o.rationale: Applicants can self-serve and vendors can provide cloud systems, call centers, analytics, payment processing, and document tools, but statutes and due-process protections require accountable government agencies to set policy, determine or oversee eligibility, issue notices and payments, hear appeals, investigate fraud, protect data, monitor delegated administrators, and answer to elected officials and courts. AI may eliminate some transactions and support roles, but it cannot fully replace the responsible public administrator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that end-to-end automated or privately delegated systems can lawfully perform high-volume eligibility, adverse actions, appeals, payments, fraud controls, and program oversight with sustained accuracy, equity, due process, and public accountability would lower the estimate.
caveats: Operational components may migrate to contractors in other industries while accountable administration remains governmental.
Program simplification or automatic enrollment could remove more administrative quantity than current technology deployments suggest.

narrative.executive_view: Human-resource program administration is highly document-, rules-, and data-intensive, creating material AI opportunity in intake, verification, correspondence, case routing, fraud controls, and contact support. Human review, due process, vulnerable populations, legacy systems, and fragmented governance limit implementation, while the government-only classification makes private-firm transfer and commercial retention undefined.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can extract evidence, check completeness, match data, summarize contacts, draft notices, answer routine questions, prioritize cases, translate communications, and flag anomalies. Complex eligibility, disability evidence, adverse actions, appeals, investigations, policy, and payment accountability require trained public officials and human review.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits appear as lower backlog, faster decisions, improved access, fewer errors, reduced improper payments, and administrative capacity. They do not flow through market pricing or EBITDA and may be reallocated to service quality or new mandates.
narrative.firm_availability: Census defines government establishments only, and the frozen dataset has neither receipts nor a defensible LMM count. Private technology, eligibility, claims, and contact-center vendors must be screened in their own industry codes.
narrative.demand_durability: Aging raises retirement and Medicare workloads, while disability, unemployment, food-assistance, and welfare caseloads vary with economic conditions and policy. Simplification and self-service may reduce effort per case, but public accountability and large recurring beneficiary populations sustain the service basket.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Risks include inaccurate or biased eligibility decisions, weak data, identity errors, privacy, cybersecurity, opaque models, improper denials or payments, appeals, public trust, procurement, legacy systems, fragmented federal-state responsibility, policy changes, and the structural mismatch with a private-company screen.
narrative.principal_driver: High-volume applications, redeterminations, records, notices, and cross-dataset checks make the administrative workflow unusually amenable to AI assistance.
narrative.principal_weakness: Government ownership eliminates the LMM acquisition, control-transfer, receipts, and commercial-retention constructs, while due process constrains automation.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 923130 Administration of Human Resource Programs
sources.supports: Defines government administration of public assistance, social work, welfare, Social Security, disability insurance, Medicare, unemployment insurance, and workers' compensation, with key exclusions.

sources.title: Occupational Employment and Wages: Eligibility Interviewers, Government Programs
sources.supports: Defines eligibility work across welfare, unemployment, Social Security, housing, and related programs and reports 150,190 workers nationally in May 2023.

sources.title: Federal Low-Income Programs: Use of Data to Verify Eligibility
sources.supports: Reports agencies using more than 30 electronic data sources across six selected programs and describes integrity and beneficiary-burden benefits and challenges of data verification.

sources.title: Fraud and Improper Payments: Data Quality and a Skilled Workforce Are Essential for Realizing AI's Benefits
sources.supports: Explains that AI can assist fraud and improper-payment work but requires reliable data, skilled staff, and a human in the loop.

sources.title: Combating Fraud: Challenges in Federally Funded, State-Administered Programs
sources.supports: Documents state eligibility and payment decisions and constraints from weak controls, limited visibility, data and system problems, and limited program-management capacity.

sources.title: Federal Telework: Social Security Administration Needs a Workforce Plan
sources.supports: Documents SSA workforce and disability-backlog pressures and reports initial-claim processing time improving from 240 to 209 days during 2025 after prior deterioration.

sources.title: Older Adults Outnumber Children in 11 States and Nearly Half of U.S. Counties
sources.supports: Reports the U.S. population age 65 and older grew 3.1% to 61.2 million from 2023 to 2024, increasing aging-related program demand.

sources.title: SNAP Data Tables
sources.supports: Provides national and state participation, household, benefit, and cost data through fiscal 2025 and monthly data into fiscal 2026, evidencing a continuing administrative workload.

sources.title: The Employment Situation — June 2026
sources.supports: Reports a 4.2% unemployment rate and 7.1 million unemployed people in June 2026, anchoring current unemployment-insurance workload context.
